question:
12. A car is being driven so that its wheels, all of radius \( a \) feet, have an angular velocity of \( \omega \) radians per second. A particle is thrown off from the tire of one of these wheels, where it is supposed that \( a \omega^{2}>g \). Neglecting the resistance of the air, show that the maximum height above the roadway which the particle can reach is
\[
\frac{\left(a \omega+g \omega^{-1}\right)^{2}}{2 g}
\]
solution:
Solution. If a particle is thrown into motion in a gravitational field starting at height \( h \) and with upward component of velocity \( v \), it will rise to the height \( h+\left(v^{2} / 2 g\right) \). [The horizontal components of the motion do not influence the maximum height].

As long as the particle remains attached to the tire, it follows the path of a cycloid, and we may take its equations of motion as
\[
\begin{array}{l}
x=a \omega t-a \sin \omega t \\
y=a(1-\cos \omega t)
\end{array}
\]

If the particle leaves the tire when \( \omega t=\theta \), then it starts into gravitational motion with height \( a(1-\cos \theta) \) and upward velocity component \( y^{\prime}= \) \( a \omega \sin \theta \). Hence it reaches the height
\[
H=a(1-\cos \theta)+\frac{a^{2} \omega^{2}}{2 g} \sin ^{2} \theta
\]
provided \( 0 \leq \theta \leq \pi \) (to ensure that the particle starts upward).
We are asked to maximize \( \boldsymbol{H} \) by choice of \( \theta \). We set
\[
\frac{d H}{d \theta}=a \sin \theta+\frac{a^{2} \omega^{2}}{g} \sin \theta \cos \theta=0
\]
and find that the critical points are \( 0, \pi, \theta_{0} \), where \( \theta_{0}=\arccos \left(-g / a \omega^{2}\right) \). (Since \( a \omega^{2}>g \), there is such a \( \theta_{0} \).) The corresponding values of \( H \) are \( 0,2 a \), and
\[
H_{0}=a\left(1+\frac{g}{a \omega^{2}}\right)+\frac{a^{2} \omega^{2}}{2 g}\left(1-\frac{g^{2}}{a^{2} \omega^{4}}\right)=\frac{1}{2 g}\left(a \omega+g \omega^{-1}\right)^{2}
\]

Since \( \left(a \omega+g \omega^{-1}\right)^{2}>4 a g, H_{0}>2 a \), so the maximum value of \( H \) is \( H_{0} \). We can also find the maximum value of \( \boldsymbol{H} \) by writing (1) in the form
\[
H=H_{0}-\frac{a^{2} \omega^{2}}{2 g}\left(\cos \theta+\frac{g}{a \omega^{2}}\right)^{2}
\]

Remark. If \( a \omega^{2} \leq g \), the maximum value of \( H \) will be \( 2 a \), attained for \( \theta=\pi \), which means that particles flying off never go higher than the top of the wheel.

descriptive_long.question:
12. A car is being driven so that its wheels, all of radius \( wheelradius \) feet, have an angular velocity of \( anglspeed \) radians per second. A particle is thrown off from the tire of one of these wheels, where it is supposed that \( wheelradius anglspeed^{2}>gravaccel \). Neglecting the resistance of the air, show that the maximum height above the roadway which the particle can reach is
\[
\frac{\left(wheelradius anglspeed+gravaccel anglspeed^{-1}\right)^{2}}{2 gravaccel}
\]
descriptive_long.solution:
Solution. If a particle is thrown into motion in a gravitational field starting at height \( startheight \) and with upward component of velocity \( upvelocity \), it will rise to the height \( startheight+\left(upvelocity^{2} / 2 gravaccel\right) \). [The horizontal components of the motion do not influence the maximum height].

As long as the particle remains attached to the tire, it follows the path of a cycloid, and we may take its equations of motion as
\[
\begin{array}{l}
horizpos=wheelradius anglspeed timevar-wheelradius \sin anglspeed timevar \\
vertpos=wheelradius(1-\cos anglspeed timevar)
\end{array}
\]

If the particle leaves the tire when \( anglspeed timevar=angletheta \), then it starts into gravitational motion with height \( wheelradius(1-\cos angletheta) \) and upward velocity component \( vertpos^{\prime}= \) \( wheelradius anglspeed \sin angletheta \). Hence it reaches the height
\[
maxheight=wheelradius(1-\cos angletheta)+\frac{wheelradius^{2} anglspeed^{2}}{2 gravaccel} \sin ^{2} angletheta
\]
provided \( 0 \leq angletheta \leq \pi \) (to ensure that the particle starts upward).
We are asked to maximize \( \boldsymbol{maxheight} \) by choice of \( angletheta \). We set
\[
\frac{d maxheight}{d angletheta}=wheelradius \sin angletheta+\frac{wheelradius^{2} anglspeed^{2}}{gravaccel} \sin angletheta \cos angletheta=0
\]
and find that the critical points are \( 0, \pi, anglecrit \), where \( anglecrit=\arccos \left(-gravaccel / wheelradius anglspeed^{2}\right) \). (Since \( wheelradius anglspeed^{2}>gravaccel \), there is such a \( anglecrit \).) The corresponding values of \( maxheight \) are \( 0,2 wheelradius \), and
\[
peakheight=wheelradius\left(1+\frac{gravaccel}{wheelradius anglspeed^{2}}\right)+\frac{wheelradius^{2} anglspeed^{2}}{2 gravaccel}\left(1-\frac{gravaccel^{2}}{wheelradius^{2} anglspeed^{4}}\right)=\frac{1}{2 gravaccel}\left(wheelradius anglspeed+gravaccel anglspeed^{-1}\right)^{2}
\]

Since \( \left(wheelradius anglspeed+gravaccel anglspeed^{-1}\right)^{2}>4 wheelradius gravaccel, peakheight>2 wheelradius \), so the maximum value of \( maxheight \) is \( peakheight \). We can also find the maximum value of \( \boldsymbol{maxheight} \) by writing (1) in the form
\[
maxheight=peakheight-\frac{wheelradius^{2} anglspeed^{2}}{2 gravaccel}\left(\cos angletheta+\frac{gravaccel}{wheelradius anglspeed^{2}}\right)^{2}
\]

Remark. If \( wheelradius anglspeed^{2} \leq gravaccel \), the maximum value of \( maxheight \) will be \( 2 wheelradius \), attained for \( angletheta=\pi \), which means that particles flying off never go higher than the top of the wheel.

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
12. A car is being driven so that its wheels, all of radius \( harvestmoon \) feet, have an angular velocity of \( silvercrest \) radians per second. A particle is thrown off from the tire of one of these wheels, where it is supposed that \( harvestmoon silvercrest^{2}>starlattice \). Neglecting the resistance of the air, show that the maximum height above the roadway which the particle can reach is
\[
\frac{\left(harvestmoon silvercrest+starlattice silvercrest^{-1}\right)^{2}}{2 starlattice}
\]
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
Solution. If a particle is thrown into motion in a gravitational field starting at height \( skylighter \) and with upward component of velocity \( moonripple \), it will rise to the height \( skylighter+\left(moonripple^{2} / 2 starlattice\right) \). [The horizontal components of the motion do not influence the maximum height].

As long as the particle remains attached to the tire, it follows the path of a cycloid, and we may take its equations of motion as
\[
\begin{array}{l}
meadowland=harvestmoon \, silvercrest \, windwhistle-harvestmoon \sin silvercrest \, windwhistle \\
riverstone=harvestmoon(1-\cos silvercrest \, windwhistle)
\end{array}
\]

If the particle leaves the tire when \( silvercrest \, windwhistle=sunsetglow \), then it starts into gravitational motion with height \( harvestmoon(1-\cos sunsetglow) \) and upward velocity component \( riverstone^{\prime}= \) \( harvestmoon \, silvercrest \sin sunsetglow \). Hence it reaches the height
\[
cloudheight=harvestmoon(1-\cos sunsetglow)+\frac{harvestmoon^{2} \, silvercrest^{2}}{2 starlattice} \sin ^{2} sunsetglow
\]
provided \( 0 \leq sunsetglow \leq \pi \) (to ensure that the particle starts upward).
We are asked to maximize \( \boldsymbol{cloudheight} \) by choice of \( sunsetglow \). We set
\[
\frac{d \, cloudheight}{d \, sunsetglow}=harvestmoon \sin sunsetglow+\frac{harvestmoon^{2} \, silvercrest^{2}}{starlattice} \sin sunsetglow \cos sunsetglow=0
\]
and find that the critical points are \( 0, \pi, dawndrift \), where \( dawndrift=\arccos \left(-starlattice / harvestmoon \, silvercrest^{2}\right) \). (Since \( harvestmoon \, silvercrest^{2}>starlattice \), there is such a \( dawndrift \).) The corresponding values of \( cloudheight \) are \( 0,2 harvestmoon \), and
\[
cobaltgrove=harvestmoon\left(1+\frac{starlattice}{harvestmoon \, silvercrest^{2}}\right)+\frac{harvestmoon^{2} \, silvercrest^{2}}{2 starlattice}\left(1-\frac{starlattice^{2}}{harvestmoon^{2} \, silvercrest^{4}}\right)=\frac{1}{2 starlattice}\left(harvestmoon \, silvercrest+starlattice \, silvercrest^{-1}\right)^{2}
\]

Since \( \left(harvestmoon \, silvercrest+starlattice \, silvercrest^{-1}\right)^{2}>4 harvestmoon starlattice, cobaltgrove>2 harvestmoon \), so the maximum value of \( cloudheight \) is \( cobaltgrove \). We can also find the maximum value of \( \boldsymbol{cloudheight} \) by writing (1) in the form
\[
cloudheight=cobaltgrove-\frac{harvestmoon^{2} \, silvercrest^{2}}{2 starlattice}\left(\cos sunsetglow+\frac{starlattice}{harvestmoon \, silvercrest^{2}}\right)^{2}
\]

Remark. If \( harvestmoon \, silvercrest^{2} \leq starlattice \), the maximum value of \( cloudheight \) will be \( 2 harvestmoon \), attained for \( sunsetglow=\pi \), which means that particles flying off never go higher than the top of the wheel.

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
12. A car is being driven so that its wheels, all of radius \( flatness \) feet, have an angular velocity of \( stillness \) radians per second. A particle is thrown off from the tire of one of these wheels, where it is supposed that \( flatness stillness^{2}>weightlessness \). Neglecting the resistance of the air, show that the maximum height above the roadway which the particle can reach is
\[
\frac{\left(flatness stillness+weightlessness stillness^{-1}\right)^{2}}{2 weightlessness}
\]
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
Solution. If a particle is thrown into motion in a gravitational field starting at height \( grounded \) and with upward component of velocity \( slowness \), it will rise to the height \( grounded+\left(slowness^{2} / 2 weightlessness\right) \). [The horizontal components of the motion do not influence the maximum height].

As long as the particle remains attached to the tire, it follows the path of a cycloid, and we may take its equations of motion as
\[
\begin{array}{l}
vertical=flatness stillness timeless-flatness \sin stillness timeless \\
horizontal=flatness(1-\cos stillness timeless)
\end{array}
\]

If the particle leaves the tire when \( stillness timeless=straight \), then it starts into gravitational motion with height \( flatness(1-\cos straight) \) and upward velocity component \( horizontal^{\prime}= flatness stillness \sin straight \). Hence it reaches the height
\[
deepness=flatness(1-\cos straight)+\frac{flatness^{2} stillness^{2}}{2 weightlessness} \sin ^{2} straight
\]
provided \( 0 \leq straight \leq \pi \) (to ensure that the particle starts upward).
We are asked to maximize \( \boldsymbol{deepness} \) by choice of \( straight \). We set
\[
\frac{d\,deepness}{d\,straight}=flatness \sin straight+\frac{flatness^{2} stillness^{2}}{weightlessness} \sin straight \cos straight=0
\]
and find that the critical points are \( 0, \pi, straight_{0} \), where \( straight_{0}=\arccos \left(-weightlessness / flatness stillness^{2}\right) \). (Since \( flatness stillness^{2}>weightlessness \), there is such a \( straight_{0} \).) The corresponding values of \( deepness \) are \( 0,2 flatness \), and
\[
depthstart=flatness\left(1+\frac{weightlessness}{flatness stillness^{2}}\right)+\frac{flatness^{2} stillness^{2}}{2 weightlessness}\left(1-\frac{weightlessness^{2}}{flatness^{2} stillness^{4}}\right)=\frac{1}{2 weightlessness}\left(flatness stillness+weightlessness stillness^{-1}\right)^{2}
\]

Since \( \left(flatness stillness+weightlessness stillness^{-1}\right)^{2}>4 flatness weightlessness, depthstart>2 flatness \), so the maximum value of \( deepness \) is \( depthstart \). We can also find the maximum value of \( \boldsymbol{deepness} \) by writing (1) in the form
\[
deepness=depthstart-\frac{flatness^{2} stillness^{2}}{2 weightlessness}\left(\cos straight+\frac{weightlessness}{flatness stillness^{2}}\right)^{2}
\]

Remark. If \( flatness stillness^{2} \leq weightlessness \), the maximum value of \( deepness \) will be \( 2 flatness \), attained for \( straight=\pi \), which means that particles flying off never go higher than the top of the wheel.

garbled_string.question:
12. A car is being driven so that its wheels, all of radius \( kwmfztrn \) feet, have an angular velocity of \( vnwpqzlk \) radians per second. A particle is thrown off from the tire of one of these wheels, where it is supposed that \( kwmfztrn vnwpqzlk^{2}>msznghqp \). Neglecting the resistance of the air, show that the maximum height above the roadway which the particle can reach is
\[
\frac{\left(kwmfztrn vnwpqzlk+msznghqp vnwpqzlk^{-1}\right)^{2}}{2 msznghqp}
\]
garbled_string.solution:
Solution. If a particle is thrown into motion in a gravitational field starting at height \( nbmcvpra \) and with upward component of velocity \( rktdlsqe \), it will rise to the height \( nbmcvpra+\left(rktdlsqe^{2} / 2 msznghqp\right) \). [The horizontal components of the motion do not influence the maximum height].

As long as the particle remains attached to the tire, it follows the path of a cycloid, and we may take its equations of motion as
\[
\begin{array}{l}
qzxwvtnp=kwmfztrn vnwpqzlk ulwpqkzo-kwmfztrn \sin vnwpqzlk ulwpqkzo \\
hjgrksla=kwmfztrn(1-\cos vnwpqzlk ulwpqkzo)
\end{array}
\]

If the particle leaves the tire when \( vnwpqzlk ulwpqkzo=prqbmvns \), then it starts into gravitational motion with height \( kwmfztrn(1-\cos prqbmvns) \) and upward velocity component \( hjgrksla^{\prime}= \) \( kwmfztrn vnwpqzlk \sin prqbmvns \). Hence it reaches the height
\[
sjhdkwpq=kwmfztrn(1-\cos prqbmvns)+\frac{kwmfztrn^{2} vnwpqzlk^{2}}{2 msznghqp} \sin ^{2} prqbmvns
\]
provided \( 0 \leq prqbmvns \leq \pi \) (to ensure that the particle starts upward).
We are asked to maximize \( \boldsymbol{sjhdkwpq} \) by choice of \( prqbmvns \). We set
\[
\frac{d sjhdkwpq}{d prqbmvns}=kwmfztrn \sin prqbmvns+\frac{kwmfztrn^{2} vnwpqzlk^{2}}{msznghqp} \sin prqbmvns \cos prqbmvns=0
\]
and find that the critical points are \( 0, \pi, flqwpdht \), where \( flqwpdht=\arccos \left(-msznghqp / kwmfztrn vnwpqzlk^{2}\right) \). (Since \( kwmfztrn vnwpqzlk^{2}>msznghqp \), there is such a \( flqwpdht \).) The corresponding values of \( sjhdkwpq \) are \( 0,2 kwmfztrn \), and
\[
gjwprkzn=kwmfztrn\left(1+\frac{msznghqp}{kwmfztrn vnwpqzlk^{2}}\right)+\frac{kwmfztrn^{2} vnwpqzlk^{2}}{2 msznghqp}\left(1-\frac{msznghqp^{2}}{kwmfztrn^{2} vnwpqzlk^{4}}\right)=\frac{1}{2 msznghqp}\left(kwmfztrn vnwpqzlk+msznghqp vnwpqzlk^{-1}\right)^{2}
\]

Since \( \left(kwmfztrn vnwpqzlk+msznghqp vnwpqzlk^{-1}\right)^{2}>4 kwmfztrn msznghqp, gjwprkzn>2 kwmfztrn \), so the maximum value of \( sjhdkwpq \) is \( gjwprkzn \). We can also find the maximum value of \( \boldsymbol{sjhdkwpq} \) by writing (1) in the form
\[
sjhdkwpq=gjwprkzn-\frac{kwmfztrn^{2} vnwpqzlk^{2}}{2 msznghqp}\left(\cos prqbmvns+\frac{msznghqp}{kwmfztrn vnwpqzlk^{2}}\right)^{2}
\]

Remark. If \( kwmfztrn vnwpqzlk^{2} \leq msznghqp \), the maximum value of \( sjhdkwpq \) will be \( 2 kwmfztrn \), attained for \( prqbmvns=\pi \), which means that particles flying off never go higher than the top of the wheel.

kernel_variant.question:
Consider a perfectly spherical rigid Earth of radius $R_{e}$ that rotates uniformly with angular velocity  

\[
\boldsymbol{\omega}_{e}
      =\omega_{e}\bigl(\cos\varphi\,\hat{\boldsymbol{\imath}}_{N}
                     +\sin\varphi\,\hat{\boldsymbol{\imath}}_{U}\bigr),
\qquad -\dfrac{\pi}{2}<\varphi<\dfrac{\pi}{2},
\]

where the right-handed orthonormal triad  
$\bigl(\hat{\boldsymbol{\imath}}_{E},
        \hat{\boldsymbol{\imath}}_{N},
        \hat{\boldsymbol{\imath}}_{U}\bigr)$
points respectively East, North and Up at the point of interest and satisfies  
$\hat{\boldsymbol{\imath}}_{E}\times\hat{\boldsymbol{\imath}}_{N}
      =\hat{\boldsymbol{\imath}}_{U}$,
$\hat{\boldsymbol{\imath}}_{N}\times\hat{\boldsymbol{\imath}}_{U}
      =\hat{\boldsymbol{\imath}}_{E}$.

A high-speed train travels \emph{due west} along the circle of latitude
$\varphi$ with ground speed $v>0$.
Each wheel is an ideal thin hoop of radius $R$ that rolls without
slipping, so its spin about the \emph{negative} north axis is  

\[
\boldsymbol{\omega}_{w}=-\alpha\,\hat{\boldsymbol{\imath}}_{N},
\qquad
\alpha=\dfrac{v}{R},
\qquad
R\alpha^{2}>g_{0},
\]
$g_{0}$ denoting the conventional surface gravity.

Inside one wheel a tiny bead is soldered to a spoke at the distance
$r=\lambda R$ from the wheel centre ($0<\lambda\le1$).  
Measured \emph{counter-clockwise from the downward vertical as viewed
from the south}, the spoke makes the angle $0\le\theta\le\pi$.  
Exactly when the spoke reaches the value $\theta$ the weld fails and the
bead is released.

The subsequent motion will be analysed in the Earth-fixed (rotating)
frame with origin at the Earth's centre $C$.  In this frame the bead
experiences  

$\bullet$ the Newtonian gravity $-g_{0}\,\hat{\boldsymbol{\imath}}_{U}$;

$\bullet$ the centrifugal acceleration
      $-\boldsymbol{\omega}_{e}\times
       (\boldsymbol{\omega}_{e}\times\mathbf r)$;

$\bullet$ the Coriolis acceleration
      $2\,\boldsymbol{\omega}_{e}\times\mathbf v$,

where $\mathbf r(t)$ and $\mathbf v(t)$ denote position and velocity in
the rotating frame.  Air resistance is neglected and the wheel is
assumed to spin so fast that the bead is ejected upwards.

Throughout we keep the \emph{two} independent dimensionless small
parameters  

\[
\varepsilon=\dfrac{\omega_{e}}{\alpha}\ll1,
\qquad
\eta=\dfrac{\omega_{e}^{2}R_{e}}{g_{0}}\approx3\times10^{-3},
\tag{$\star$}
\]
retain every contribution of order $\varepsilon$ or $\eta$ and discard
all products or squares
$\varepsilon^{2},\,\eta^{2},\,\varepsilon\eta$.

Useful abbreviation (effective gravity at latitude $\varphi$):

\[
g=g_{0}-\omega_{e}^{2}R_{e}\cos^{2}\varphi
  =g_{0}\bigl(1-\eta\cos^{2}\varphi\bigr).
\tag{$\dagger$}
\]

Tasks  

(a)  In the local basis
$\bigl(\hat{\boldsymbol{\imath}}_{E},
       \hat{\boldsymbol{\imath}}_{N},
       \hat{\boldsymbol{\imath}}_{U}\bigr)$
determine the bead's initial position $\mathbf r_{0}$ (with respect to
$C$) and its \emph{rotating-frame} velocity $\mathbf v_{0}$,
retaining all terms up to $O(\varepsilon)$ and $O(\eta)$ and expressing
your answer in $R_{e},R,\lambda,\theta,\alpha,\omega_{e}$.

(b)  Let
$L_{N}=m\,\hat{\boldsymbol{\imath}}_{N}\!\cdot(\mathbf r\times\mathbf v)$
be the north component of the angular momentum in the rotating frame.

(i)  Prove that  

\[
\dfrac{{\mathrm d}L_{N}}{{\mathrm d}t}
      =m\,g\,r_{E}(t)
       +O\bigl(\varepsilon\,m g R\bigr)
       +O\bigl(\eta\,m g R\bigr),
\qquad
r_{E}(t)=\hat{\boldsymbol{\imath}}_{E}\!\cdot\mathbf r(t),
\]

and that over the whole flight  

\[
\dfrac{L_{N}(t)-L_{N}(0)}{L_{N}(0)}
      =O\!\Bigl(\dfrac{R}{R_{e}}\Bigr)+O(\varepsilon)+O(\eta).
\]

(ii)  Hence show that the trajectory departs from a single vertical
plane by at most $O\!\bigl(R/R_{e}\bigr)+O(\varepsilon)+O(\eta)$ and
that, to the same order, this plane coincides with the local meridian.

(c)  Working consistently to $O(\varepsilon)$ and $O(\eta)$, prove that
the greatest height attained by the bead above the rail top is  

\[
h(\lambda,\theta)=
R-\lambda R\cos\theta
+\dfrac{\lambda^{2}R^{2}\alpha^{2}\sin^{2}\theta}{2g}
\bigl[1+2\varepsilon\cos\varphi\bigr]
+O\!\bigl(\varepsilon^{2},\eta^{2}\bigr).
\]

(d)  For fixed $\lambda$ maximise $h(\lambda,\theta)$ with respect
to $\theta$ and show that  

\[
\boxed{\;
\cos\theta^{\!*}
   =-\dfrac{g}{\lambda R\alpha^{2}}
     \Bigl[1-2\varepsilon\cos\varphi+O(\eta)\Bigr]\;},
\]
\[
\boxed{%
h_{\max}(\lambda)=
R+\dfrac{g}{2\alpha^{2}}\bigl[1-2\varepsilon\cos\varphi\bigr]
+\dfrac{\lambda^{2}R^{2}\alpha^{2}}{2g}
\bigl[1+2\varepsilon\cos\varphi\bigr]
+O\!\bigl(\varepsilon^{2},\eta^{2}\bigr)}.
\]

(e)  Finally maximise $h_{\max}(\lambda)$ over
$0<\lambda\le1$ and prove that \emph{every} bead satisfies  

\[
\boxed{%
h\le
\dfrac{\bigl(R\alpha+g/\alpha\bigr)^{2}}{2g}
+\varepsilon\cos\varphi\,
\dfrac{R^{2}\alpha^{2}-g^{2}/\alpha^{2}}{g}
+O\!\bigl(\varepsilon^{2},\eta^{2}\bigr)},
\]

with equality iff $\lambda=1$ and  

\[
\cos\theta=-\,\dfrac{g}{R\alpha^{2}}
           \Bigl[1-2\varepsilon\cos\varphi+O(\eta)\Bigr].
\]

(f)  Give a qualitative explanation showing why the Coriolis force
contributes only at $O(\varepsilon)$ to the maximum height, whereas the
centrifugal term already enters at $O(\eta)$, and discuss the relative
magnitude of the two effects for realistic high-speed wheels.



%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
kernel_variant.solution:
Throughout the solution we strictly enforce the double-expansion rule
$(\star)$: every $O(\varepsilon)$ and every $O(\eta)$ contribution is
retained, whereas all products
$\varepsilon^{2},\ \eta^{2},\ \varepsilon\eta$ are discarded.

----------------------------------------------------------------
(a)  Initial data  

Position of the wheel centre in the rotating frame:  
$\mathbf r_{C}=(R_{e}+R)\,\hat{\boldsymbol{\imath}}_{U}$.

Bead offset (adopted $\theta$-convention):

\[
\mathbf r'=\lambda R
\bigl(\sin\theta\,\hat{\boldsymbol{\imath}}_{E}
      -\cos\theta\,\hat{\boldsymbol{\imath}}_{U}\bigr).
\]

Hence  

\[
\boxed{\;
\mathbf r_{0}=\mathbf r_{C}+\mathbf r'
=\bigl(R_{e}+R-\lambda R\cos\theta\bigr)\hat{\boldsymbol{\imath}}_{U}
+\lambda R\sin\theta\,\hat{\boldsymbol{\imath}}_{E}}\!.
\tag{1}
\]

Velocity in the rotating frame comes from three contributions.

(i)  \emph{Translation of the wheel centre}  
(the ground-frame speed is purely westward)
\[
\mathbf v_{C}=-v\,\hat{\boldsymbol{\imath}}_{E}
             =-\alpha R\,\hat{\boldsymbol{\imath}}_{E}.
\]

(ii)  \emph{Spin of the wheel}
\[
\mathbf v_{\text{spin}}
   =\boldsymbol{\omega}_{w}\times\mathbf r'
   =\alpha\lambda R
      \bigl(\cos\theta\,\hat{\boldsymbol{\imath}}_{E}
           +\sin\theta\,\hat{\boldsymbol{\imath}}_{U}\bigr).
\]

(iii)  \emph{Transformation from the inertial to the rotating frame.}  
Because the centre-of-mass term has already been converted in (i), only
$\mathbf r'$ contributes:

\[
-\boldsymbol{\omega}_{e}\times\mathbf r'
   =-\omega_{e}\lambda R
      \Bigl(\cos\varphi\cos\theta\,\hat{\boldsymbol{\imath}}_{E}
           -\sin\varphi\sin\theta\,\hat{\boldsymbol{\imath}}_{N}
           +\cos\varphi\sin\theta\,\hat{\boldsymbol{\imath}}_{U}\Bigr).
\]

Collecting (i)-(iii) and introducing
$\varepsilon=\omega_{e}/\alpha$ gives

\[
\boxed{
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf v_{0}&=
\alpha R\Bigl[
\bigl(-1+\lambda\cos\theta
      +\varepsilon\lambda\cos\varphi\cos\theta\bigr)\,
          \hat{\boldsymbol{\imath}}_{E} \\
&\quad -\varepsilon\lambda\sin\varphi\sin\theta\,
          \hat{\boldsymbol{\imath}}_{N}
\;+\;\lambda\sin\theta
      \bigl(1+\varepsilon\cos\varphi\bigr)\,
          \hat{\boldsymbol{\imath}}_{U}\Bigr]
+O\!\bigl(\varepsilon^{2}\alpha R\bigr).
\end{aligned}}
\tag{2}
\]

Note especially the \emph{non-vanishing} north component
$(v_{0})_{N}=-\varepsilon\alpha\lambda R\sin\theta\sin\varphi
              =O(\varepsilon\alpha R)$.

----------------------------------------------------------------
(b)  Near-conservation of $L_{N}$ and quasi-planarity  

Define $L_{N}=m\,\hat{\boldsymbol{\imath}}_{N}\!\cdot(\mathbf r\times\mathbf v)$.
In the rotating frame  

\[
\mathbf a=-g_{0}\hat{\boldsymbol{\imath}}_{U}
-\boldsymbol{\omega}_{e}\times
      (\boldsymbol{\omega}_{e}\times\mathbf r)
+2\,\boldsymbol{\omega}_{e}\times\mathbf v .
\]

Therefore  

\[
\dfrac{{\mathrm d}L_{N}}{{\mathrm d}t}
      =m\,\hat{\boldsymbol{\imath}}_{N}\!\cdot(\mathbf r\times\mathbf a)
      =\tau_{g}+\tau_{c}+\tau_{\text{Cor}}.
\tag{3}
\]

Gravitational torque:  
\[
\tau_{g}=m\,g\,r_{E}(t)+O(\eta\,m g R), \qquad\text{cf.\ }(\dagger).
\]

Centrifugal torque:  
$\tau_{c}=O(\eta\,m g R)$.

Coriolis torque:
$\tau_{\text{Cor}}
       =O(\varepsilon\,m g R)$
because $|\mathbf v|=O(\alpha R)$.

Collecting:

\[
\boxed{\dfrac{{\mathrm d}L_{N}}{{\mathrm d}t}
      =m\,g\,r_{E}(t)
       +O\!\bigl(\varepsilon\,m g R\bigr)
       +O\!\bigl(\eta\,m g R\bigr)}.
\]

Flight time $T$ is of order  
$T\sim2(v_{0})_{U}/g=O(\alpha\lambda R/g)$.  
Hence

\[
L_{N}(t)-L_{N}(0)
      =O\!\bigl((\varepsilon+\eta)\,m g R T\bigr)
      =O\!\bigl((\varepsilon+\eta)\,m\alpha R^{2}\bigr).
\]

From (1)-(2)

\[
L_{N}(0)=m\bigl(r_{E}v_{U}-r_{U}v_{E}\bigr)_{t=0}
        =m\alpha R R_{e}
          \Bigl[1-\lambda\cos\theta+O(\varepsilon)\Bigr],
\]
so that

\[
\boxed{\;
\frac{L_{N}(t)-L_{N}(0)}{L_{N}(0)}
          =O\!\Bigl(\dfrac{R}{R_{e}}\Bigr)+O(\varepsilon)+O(\eta)}.
\]

Since $(v_{0})_{N}=O(\varepsilon)$, the initial departure
from the meridian plane is already $O(\varepsilon)$ and, by the above
bound, does not grow beyond
$O(R/R_{e})+O(\varepsilon)+O(\eta)$ during the flight, proving (ii).

----------------------------------------------------------------
(c)  Maximum height for fixed $(\lambda,\theta)$  

The Coriolis force does no work.  
The centrifugal force is conservative, its potential being
$\tfrac12\omega_{e}^{2}r_{\perp}^{2}$, so the vertical dynamics is the
usual one but with the \emph{effective} gravity $g$ of $(\dagger)$.
Hence mechanical energy in the vertical direction is conserved to
$O(\varepsilon)$ and $O(\eta)$.

Initial height above the rail:
$z_{0}=R-\lambda R\cos\theta$.

Upward velocity component from (2):

\[
(v_{0})_{U}=\alpha\lambda R\sin\theta
            \bigl(1+\varepsilon\cos\varphi\bigr).
\]

Therefore  

\[
\dfrac{(v_{0})_{U}^{2}}{2g}
        =\dfrac{\lambda^{2}R^{2}\alpha^{2}\sin^{2}\theta}{2g}
          \bigl[1+2\varepsilon\cos\varphi\bigr].
\]

Adding the initial height gives

\[
\boxed{%
h(\lambda,\theta)=
R-\lambda R\cos\theta
+\dfrac{\lambda^{2}R^{2}\alpha^{2}\sin^{2}\theta}{2g}
\bigl[1+2\varepsilon\cos\varphi\bigr]
+O\!\bigl(\varepsilon^{2},\eta^{2}\bigr)}.
\tag{4}
\]

----------------------------------------------------------------
(d)  Maximisation with respect to $\theta$  

Differentiate (4) and set to zero:

\[
0=\lambda R\sin\theta
   \Bigl[1+\dfrac{\lambda R\alpha^{2}}{g}
         \bigl(1+2\varepsilon\cos\varphi\bigr)\cos\theta\Bigr].
\]

The non-trivial root is  

\[
\boxed{\;
\cos\theta^{\!*}
   =-\dfrac{g}{\lambda R\alpha^{2}}
     \Bigl[1-2\varepsilon\cos\varphi+O(\eta)\Bigr]\;}.
\tag{5}
\]

Insert (5) into (4).  A straightforward algebraic expansion yields  

\[
\boxed{%
h_{\max}(\lambda)=
R+\dfrac{g}{2\alpha^{2}}\bigl[1-2\varepsilon\cos\varphi\bigr]
+\dfrac{\lambda^{2}R^{2}\alpha^{2}}{2g}
\bigl[1+2\varepsilon\cos\varphi\bigr]
+O\!\bigl(\varepsilon^{2},\eta^{2}\bigr)}.
\tag{6}
\]

----------------------------------------------------------------
(e)  Maximisation with respect to $\lambda$  

From (6)

\[
\dfrac{{\mathrm d}h_{\max}}{{\mathrm d}\lambda}
   =\dfrac{\lambda R^{2}\alpha^{2}}{g}
     \bigl[1+2\varepsilon\cos\varphi\bigr]>0,
     \qquad(0<\lambda\le1),
\]
so $h_{\max}$ is strictly increasing and attains its absolute maximum at
$\lambda=1$.  Substituting $\lambda=1$ into (6) gives  

\[
h_{\text{top}}
    =R+\dfrac{g}{2\alpha^{2}}\bigl[1-2\varepsilon\cos\varphi\bigr]
     +\dfrac{R^{2}\alpha^{2}}{2g}
      \bigl[1+2\varepsilon\cos\varphi\bigr]
     =\dfrac{(R\alpha+g/\alpha)^{2}}{2g}
      +\varepsilon\cos\varphi\,
       \dfrac{R^{2}\alpha^{2}-g^{2}/\alpha^{2}}{g}.
\tag{7}
\]

Because
$h_{\max}(\lambda)\le h_{\text{top}}$ for every
$0<\lambda\le1$, we obtain the bound stated in part (e):

\[
\boxed{%
h\le
\dfrac{\bigl(R\alpha+g/\alpha\bigr)^{2}}{2g}
+\varepsilon\cos\varphi\,
\dfrac{R^{2}\alpha^{2}-g^{2}/\alpha^{2}}{g}
+O\!\bigl(\varepsilon^{2},\eta^{2}\bigr)}.
\]

Equality holds iff $\lambda=1$ and $\theta=\theta^{\!*}$, i.e.\
\[
\cos\theta=-\,\dfrac{g}{R\alpha^{2}}
           \Bigl[1-2\varepsilon\cos\varphi+O(\eta)\Bigr].
\]

----------------------------------------------------------------
(f)  Discussion of rotational corrections  

The Coriolis force is always orthogonal to the instantaneous velocity
and therefore does \emph{no} work.  Its influence on the maximum height
can arise only indirectly through tiny plane-change effects and is
consequently suppressed to $O(\varepsilon)$.  

The centrifugal force, on the other hand, derives from the potential
$\tfrac12\omega_{e}^{2}r_{\perp}^{2}$ and its vertical component
constitutes a genuine reduction of the effective gravity by a fraction
$\eta\cos^{2}\varphi$.  Since $\eta\approx3\times10^{-3}$ whereas
$\varepsilon=\omega_{e}/\alpha\lesssim10^{-6}$ for realistic
high-speed wheels ($\alpha\gtrsim10^{2}\,\mathrm{s}^{-1}$),
centrifugal corrections exceed Coriolis corrections by three orders of
magnitude and thus provide the first measurable rotational effect on the
maximum height.



%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
1.  Three–dimensional setting.  While the core idea (a bead detaching from a rolling wheel) is retained, the motion now occurs on a rotating spherical Earth, demanding full vector treatment instead of plane kinematics.
2.  Additional forces.  Besides gravity the solver must handle centrifugal and Coriolis terms and decide which ones influence the height to a given order.
3.  Small–parameter perturbation.  A systematic expansion in ε = ωₑ/α is required; this calls for perturbative reasoning rather than purely algebraic manipulation.
4.  Conservation laws in non-inertial frames.  The candidate must know which inertial forces do or do not perform work and exploit that to keep the calculation manageable.
5.  Two layers of optimisation.  First θ must be chosen to maximise the altitude for each λ, then λ itself must be optimised.  Because λ enters both algebraically and through the root of a transcendental equation, careful order‐keeping in ε is essential.
6.  Geometric insight.  Showing that the path lies in a tilted plane (part (b)) demands recognition of an almost-conserved component of angular momentum, a concept absent from the original problem.

All these elements go well beyond the planar cycloid of the original exercise, making the enhanced variant significantly harder and richer in mathematical structure.

original_kernel_variant.question:
Consider a perfectly spherical rigid Earth of radius $R_{e}$ that
rotates uniformly with angular velocity  

\[
\boldsymbol{\omega}_{e}
      =\omega_{e}\bigl(\cos\varphi\,\hat{\boldsymbol{\imath}}_{N}
                     +\sin\varphi\,\hat{\boldsymbol{\imath}}_{U}\bigr),
\qquad -\dfrac{\pi}{2}<\varphi<\dfrac{\pi}{2},
\]

where the right-handed orthonormal triad
$\bigl(\hat{\boldsymbol{\imath}}_{E},
        \hat{\boldsymbol{\imath}}_{N},
        \hat{\boldsymbol{\imath}}_{U}\bigr)$
points respectively East, North and Up at the point of interest and
satisfies
$\hat{\boldsymbol{\imath}}_{E}\times\hat{\boldsymbol{\imath}}_{N}
      =\hat{\boldsymbol{\imath}}_{U}$,
$\hat{\boldsymbol{\imath}}_{N}\times\hat{\boldsymbol{\imath}}_{U}
      =\hat{\boldsymbol{\imath}}_{E}$.

A high-speed train travels {\em due west} along the circle of latitude
$\varphi$ with ground speed $v>0$.
Each wheel is an ideal thin hoop of radius $R$ that rolls without
slipping, so its spin about the {\em negative} north axis is  

\[
\boldsymbol{\omega}_{w}=-\alpha\,\hat{\boldsymbol{\imath}}_{N},
\qquad
\alpha=\dfrac{v}{R},
\qquad
R\alpha^{2}>g_{0},
\]
$g_{0}$ denoting the conventional surface gravity.

Inside one wheel a tiny bead is soldered to a spoke at the distance
$r=\lambda R$ from the wheel centre ($0<\lambda\le1$).  
Measured {\em counter-clockwise from the downward vertical as viewed
from the south}, the spoke makes the angle $0\le\theta\le\pi$.  
Exactly when the spoke reaches the value $\theta$ the weld fails and the
bead is released.

The subsequent motion will be analysed in the Earth-fixed (rotating)
frame with origin at the Earth's centre $C$.  In this frame the bead
experiences

$\bullet$ the Newtonian gravity $-g_{0}\,\hat{\boldsymbol{\imath}}_{U}$;

$\bullet$ the centrifugal acceleration
      $-\boldsymbol{\omega}_{e}\times
       (\boldsymbol{\omega}_{e}\times\mathbf r)$;

$\bullet$ the Coriolis acceleration
      $2\,\boldsymbol{\omega}_{e}\times\mathbf v$,

where $\mathbf r(t)$ and $\mathbf v(t)$ denote position and velocity in
the rotating frame.  Air resistance is neglected and the wheel is
assumed to spin so fast that the bead is ejected upwards.

Throughout we keep the {\em two} independent dimensionless small
parameters  

\[
\varepsilon=\dfrac{\omega_{e}}{\alpha}\ll1,
\qquad
\eta=\dfrac{\omega_{e}^{2}R_{e}}{g_{0}}\approx3\times10^{-3},
\tag{$\star$}
\]
retain every contribution of order $\varepsilon$ or $\eta$ and discard
all products or squares
$\varepsilon^{2},\,\eta^{2},\,\varepsilon\eta$.

Useful abbreviation (effective gravity at latitude $\varphi$):

\[
g=g_{0}-\omega_{e}^{2}R_{e}\cos^{2}\varphi
  =g_{0}\bigl(1-\eta\cos^{2}\varphi\bigr).
\tag{$\dagger$}
\]

Tasks  

(a)  In the local basis
$\bigl(\hat{\boldsymbol{\imath}}_{E},
       \hat{\boldsymbol{\imath}}_{N},
       \hat{\boldsymbol{\imath}}_{U}\bigr)$
determine the bead's initial position $\mathbf r_{0}$ (with respect to
$C$) and its {\em rotating-frame} velocity $\mathbf v_{0}$,
retaining all terms up to $O(\varepsilon)$ and $O(\eta)$ and expressing
your answer in $R_{e},R,\lambda,\theta,\alpha,\omega_{e}$.

(b)  Let
$L_{N}=m\,\hat{\boldsymbol{\imath}}_{N}\!\cdot(\mathbf r\times\mathbf v)$
be the north component of the angular momentum in the rotating frame.

(i)  Prove that  

\[
\dfrac{{\mathrm d}L_{N}}{{\mathrm d}t}
      =m\,g\,r_{E}(t)+O\bigl(\varepsilon\,m g R\bigr),
\qquad
r_{E}(t)=\hat{\boldsymbol{\imath}}_{E}\!\cdot\mathbf r(t),
\]

and that over the whole flight  

\[
\dfrac{L_{N}(t)-L_{N}(0)}{L_{N}(0)}
      =O\!\Bigl(\dfrac{R}{R_{e}}\Bigr)+O(\varepsilon).
\]

(ii)  Hence show that the trajectory departs from a single vertical
plane by at most $O\!\bigl(R/R_{e}\bigr)+O(\varepsilon)$ and that, to the
same order, this plane coincides with the local meridian.

(c)  Working consistently to $O(\varepsilon)$ and $O(\eta)$, prove that
the greatest height attained by the bead above the rail top is  

\[
h(\lambda,\theta)=
R-\lambda R\cos\theta
+\dfrac{\lambda^{2}R^{2}\alpha^{2}\sin^{2}\theta}{2g}
\bigl[1+2\varepsilon\cos\varphi\bigr]
+O\!\bigl(\varepsilon^{2},\eta^{2}\bigr).
\]

(d)  For fixed $\lambda$ maximise $h(\lambda,\theta)$ with respect
to $\theta$ and show that  

\[
\boxed{\;
\cos\theta^{\!*}
   =-\dfrac{g}{\lambda R\alpha^{2}}
     \Bigl[1-2\varepsilon\cos\varphi+O(\eta)\Bigr]\;},
\]
\[
\boxed{%
h_{\max}(\lambda)=
R+\dfrac{g}{2\alpha^{2}}\bigl[1-2\varepsilon\cos\varphi\bigr]
+\dfrac{\lambda^{2}R^{2}\alpha^{2}}{2g}
\bigl[1+2\varepsilon\cos\varphi\bigr]
+O\!\bigl(\varepsilon^{2},\eta^{2}\bigr)}.
\]

(e)  Finally maximise $h_{\max}(\lambda)$ over
$0<\lambda\le1$ and prove that {\em every} bead satisfies  

\[
\boxed{%
h\le
\dfrac{\bigl(R\alpha+g/\alpha\bigr)^{2}}{2g}
+\varepsilon\cos\varphi\,
\dfrac{R^{2}\alpha^{2}-g^{2}/\alpha^{2}}{2g}
+O\!\bigl(\varepsilon^{2},\eta^{2}\bigr)},
\]

with equality iff $\lambda=1$ and  

\[
\cos\theta=-\,\dfrac{g}{R\alpha^{2}}
           \Bigl[1-2\varepsilon\cos\varphi+O(\eta)\Bigr].
\]

(f)  Give a qualitative explanation showing why the Coriolis force
contributes only at $O(\varepsilon)$ to the maximum height, whereas the
centrifugal term already enters at $O(\eta)$, and discuss the relative
magnitude of the two effects for realistic high-speed wheels.



%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
original_kernel_variant.solution:
Throughout we enforce the double-expansion rule $(\star)$, i.e.\
all $O(\varepsilon)$ and all $O(\eta)$ terms are retained whereas
$\varepsilon^{2},\,\eta^{2}$ and $\varepsilon\eta$ are discarded.

----------------------------------------------------------------
(a)  Initial data  

Position of the wheel centre in the rotating frame:  
$\mathbf r_{C}=(R_{e}+R)\,\hat{\boldsymbol{\imath}}_{U}.$

Bead offset (adopted $\theta$-convention):

\[
\mathbf r'=\lambda R
\bigl(\sin\theta\,\hat{\boldsymbol{\imath}}_{E}
      -\cos\theta\,\hat{\boldsymbol{\imath}}_{U}\bigr).
\]

Hence  

\[
\boxed{\;
\mathbf r_{0}=\mathbf r_{C}+\mathbf r'
=\bigl(R_{e}+R-\lambda R\cos\theta\bigr)\hat{\boldsymbol{\imath}}_{U}
+\lambda R\sin\theta\,\hat{\boldsymbol{\imath}}_{E}}\!.
\tag{1}
\]

Velocity in the rotating frame.  
Three contributions are added:

(i)  {\em Translation of the wheel centre}  
(the ground-frame speed is purely westward)
\[
\mathbf v_{C}=-v\,\hat{\boldsymbol{\imath}}_{E}
             =-\alpha R\,\hat{\boldsymbol{\imath}}_{E}.
\]

(ii)  {\em Spin of the wheel}
\[
\mathbf v_{\text{spin}}
   =\boldsymbol{\omega}_{w}\times\mathbf r'
   =\alpha\lambda R
      \bigl(\cos\theta\,\hat{\boldsymbol{\imath}}_{E}
           +\sin\theta\,\hat{\boldsymbol{\imath}}_{U}\bigr).
\]

(iii)  {\em Transformation from the inertial to the rotating frame.}  
Because the centre-of-mass term has already been converted in (i), only
the offset $\mathbf r'$ contributes:

\[
-\boldsymbol{\omega}_{e}\times\mathbf r'
   =-\omega_{e}\lambda R
      \Bigl(\cos\varphi\cos\theta\,\hat{\boldsymbol{\imath}}_{E}
           -\sin\varphi\sin\theta\,\hat{\boldsymbol{\imath}}_{N}
           +\cos\varphi\sin\theta\,\hat{\boldsymbol{\imath}}_{U}\Bigr).
\]

Collecting (i)-(iii) and introducing
$\varepsilon=\omega_{e}/\alpha$ gives

\[
\boxed{
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf v_{0}&=
\alpha R\Bigl[
\bigl(-1+\lambda\cos\theta
      +\varepsilon\lambda\cos\varphi\cos\theta\bigr)\,
          \hat{\boldsymbol{\imath}}_{E} \\
&\quad -\varepsilon\lambda\sin\varphi\sin\theta\,
          \hat{\boldsymbol{\imath}}_{N}
\;+\;\lambda\sin\theta
      \bigl(1+\varepsilon\cos\varphi\bigr)\,
          \hat{\boldsymbol{\imath}}_{U}\Bigr]
+O\!\bigl(\varepsilon^{2}\alpha R\bigr).
\end{aligned}}
\tag{2}
\]

Note especially the {\em non-vanishing} north component
$(v_{0})_{N}=-\varepsilon\alpha\lambda R\sin\theta\sin\varphi=O(\varepsilon\alpha R)$.

----------------------------------------------------------------
(b)  Near-conservation of $L_{N}$ and quasi-planarity  

Define $L_{N}=m\,\hat{\boldsymbol{\imath}}_{N}\!\cdot(\mathbf r\times\mathbf v)$.
In the rotating frame  

\[
\mathbf a=-g_{0}\hat{\boldsymbol{\imath}}_{U}
-\boldsymbol{\omega}_{e}\times
      (\boldsymbol{\omega}_{e}\times\mathbf r)
+2\,\boldsymbol{\omega}_{e}\times\mathbf v .
\]

Therefore  

\[
\dfrac{{\mathrm d}L_{N}}{{\mathrm d}t}
      =m\,\hat{\boldsymbol{\imath}}_{N}\!\cdot(\mathbf r\times\mathbf a)
      =\tau_{g}+\tau_{c}+\tau_{\text{Cor}}.
\tag{3}
\]

Gravitational torque:  
$\tau_{g}=m\,g\,r_{E}(t)+O(\eta\,m g R)$, cf.\ $(\dagger)$.

Centrifugal torque:  
$\tau_{c}=O(\eta\,m g R)$.

Coriolis torque:
$\tau_{\text{Cor}}=
m\,\mathbf r\times(2\boldsymbol{\omega}_{e}\times\mathbf v)
       =O(\varepsilon\,m g R)$
because $|\mathbf v|=O(\alpha R)$.

Thus  

\[
\dfrac{{\mathrm d}L_{N}}{{\mathrm d}t}
      =m\,g\,r_{E}(t)+O\!\bigl(\varepsilon\,m g R\bigr),
\]
establishing (i).  Over the characteristic flight time
$T=2(v_{0})_{U}/g=O(\alpha\lambda R/g)$ the accumulated change obeys

\[
L_{N}(t)-L_{N}(0)
          =O\!\bigl(mgRT\bigr)
          =O\!\bigl(m\alpha R^{2}\bigr).
\]

From (1)-(2)

\[
L_{N}(0)=m\bigl(r_{E}v_{U}-r_{U}v_{E}\bigr)_{t=0}
        =m\alpha R R_{e}
          \Bigl[1-\lambda\cos\theta+O(\varepsilon)\Bigr],
\]
so that

\[
\frac{L_{N}(t)-L_{N}(0)}{L_{N}(0)}
          =O\!\Bigl(\dfrac{R}{R_{e}}\Bigr)+O(\varepsilon),
\]
as claimed.  Since $(v_{0})_{N}=O(\varepsilon)$, the initial departure
from the meridian plane is already $O(\varepsilon)$ and does not grow
beyond that order during the flight, proving (ii).

----------------------------------------------------------------
(c)  Maximum height for fixed $(\lambda,\theta)$  

The Coriolis force does no work.  
The centrifugal force is conservative, its potential being
$\tfrac12\omega_{e}^{2}r_{\perp}^{2}$, so the vertical dynamics is the
usual one but with the {\em effective} gravity $g$ of $(\dagger)$.
Hence mechanical energy in the vertical direction is conserved to
$O(\varepsilon)$ and $O(\eta)$.

Initial height above the rail:
$z_{0}=R-\lambda R\cos\theta$.

Upward velocity component from (2):

\[
(v_{0})_{U}=\alpha\lambda R\sin\theta
            \bigl(1+\varepsilon\cos\varphi\bigr).
\]

Therefore  

\[
\dfrac{(v_{0})_{U}^{2}}{2g}
        =\dfrac{\lambda^{2}R^{2}\alpha^{2}\sin^{2}\theta}{2g}
          \bigl[1+2\varepsilon\cos\varphi\bigr].
\]

Adding the initial height gives

\[
\boxed{%
h(\lambda,\theta)=
R-\lambda R\cos\theta
+\dfrac{\lambda^{2}R^{2}\alpha^{2}\sin^{2}\theta}{2g}
\bigl[1+2\varepsilon\cos\varphi\bigr]
+O\!\bigl(\varepsilon^{2},\eta^{2}\bigr)}.
\tag{4}
\]

----------------------------------------------------------------
(d)  Maximisation with respect to $\theta$  

Differentiate (4) and set to zero:

\[
0=\lambda R\sin\theta
   \Bigl[1+\dfrac{\lambda R\alpha^{2}}{g}
         \bigl(1+2\varepsilon\cos\varphi\bigr)\cos\theta\Bigr].
\]

The non-trivial root is  

\[
\boxed{\;
\cos\theta^{\!*}
   =-\dfrac{g}{\lambda R\alpha^{2}}
     \Bigl[1-2\varepsilon\cos\varphi+O(\eta)\Bigr]\;}.
\tag{5}
\]

Insert (5) into (4).  A straightforward algebraic expansion yields  

\[
\boxed{%
h_{\max}(\lambda)=
R+\dfrac{g}{2\alpha^{2}}\bigl[1-2\varepsilon\cos\varphi\bigr]
+\dfrac{\lambda^{2}R^{2}\alpha^{2}}{2g}
\bigl[1+2\varepsilon\cos\varphi\bigr]
+O\!\bigl(\varepsilon^{2},\eta^{2}\bigr)}.
\tag{6}
\]

----------------------------------------------------------------
(e)  Maximisation with respect to $\lambda$  

From (6)

\[
\dfrac{{\mathrm d}h_{\max}}{{\mathrm d}\lambda}
   =\dfrac{\lambda R^{2}\alpha^{2}}{g}
     \bigl[1+2\varepsilon\cos\varphi\bigr]>0
     \quad(0<\lambda\le1),
\]
so $h_{\max}$ is strictly increasing and attains its absolute maximum at
$\lambda=1$.  Substituting $\lambda=1$ into (6) gives  

\[
h_{\text{top}}
    =R+\dfrac{g}{2\alpha^{2}}\bigl[1-2\varepsilon\cos\varphi\bigr]
     +\dfrac{R^{2}\alpha^{2}}{2g}
      \bigl[1+2\varepsilon\cos\varphi\bigr]
     =\dfrac{(R\alpha+g/\alpha)^{2}}{2g}
      +\varepsilon\cos\varphi\,
       \dfrac{R^{2}\alpha^{2}-g^{2}/\alpha^{2}}{2g}.
\]

Because
$h_{\max}(\lambda)\le h_{\text{top}}$ for every
$0<\lambda\le1$, we obtain

\[
\boxed{%
h\le
\dfrac{\bigl(R\alpha+g/\alpha\bigr)^{2}}{2g}
+\varepsilon\cos\varphi\,
\dfrac{R^{2}\alpha^{2}-g^{2}/\alpha^{2}}{2g}
+O\!\bigl(\varepsilon^{2},\eta^{2}\bigr)}.
\]

Equality holds iff $\lambda=1$ and $\theta=\theta^{\!*}$, i.e.\
\[
\cos\theta=-\,\dfrac{g}{R\alpha^{2}}
           \Bigl[1-2\varepsilon\cos\varphi+O(\eta)\Bigr].
\]

----------------------------------------------------------------
(f)  Discussion of rotational corrections  

The Coriolis force is always orthogonal to the instantaneous velocity
and therefore does {\em no} work.  Its influence on the maximum height
can arise only indirectly through tiny plane-change effects and is
consequently suppressed to $O(\varepsilon)$.  
The centrifugal force, on the other hand, derives from the potential
$\tfrac12\omega_{e}^{2}r_{\perp}^{2}$ and its vertical component
constitutes a genuine reduction of the effective gravity by a fraction
$\eta\cos^{2}\varphi$.  Since $\eta\approx3\times10^{-3}$ whereas
$\varepsilon=\omega_{e}/\alpha\lesssim10^{-6}$ for realistic
high-speed wheels ($\alpha\gtrsim10^{2}\text{ s}^{-1}$),
centrifugal corrections exceed Coriolis corrections by three orders of
magnitude and thus provide the first measurable rotational effect on the
maximum height.



%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
1.  Three–dimensional setting.  While the core idea (a bead detaching from a rolling wheel) is retained, the motion now occurs on a rotating spherical Earth, demanding full vector treatment instead of plane kinematics.
2.  Additional forces.  Besides gravity the solver must handle centrifugal and Coriolis terms and decide which ones influence the height to a given order.
3.  Small–parameter perturbation.  A systematic expansion in ε = ωₑ/α is required; this calls for perturbative reasoning rather than purely algebraic manipulation.
4.  Conservation laws in non-inertial frames.  The candidate must know which inertial forces do or do not perform work and exploit that to keep the calculation manageable.
5.  Two layers of optimisation.  First θ must be chosen to maximise the altitude for each λ, then λ itself must be optimised.  Because λ enters both algebraically and through the root of a transcendental equation, careful order‐keeping in ε is essential.
6.  Geometric insight.  Showing that the path lies in a tilted plane (part (b)) demands recognition of an almost-conserved component of angular momentum, a concept absent from the original problem.

All these elements go well beyond the planar cycloid of the original exercise, making the enhanced variant significantly harder and richer in mathematical structure.
